meta.title: Next.js
meta.description: Next.js applications with App Router, server components, and data fetching

items.text: Do files follow App Router conventions (page.tsx, layout.tsx, loading.tsx, error.tsx)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Next.js App Router uses file-system conventions for routing. Special files like page.tsx, layout.tsx, loading.tsx, error.tsx, and not-found.tsx have specific roles.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Misnamed files won't be recognized by Next.js. A component in the wrong file won't render as a page or layout.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that route segments use the correct file names. Verify layouts wrap their children properly and don't re-render unnecessarily.
baseContent.exampleComment: This component is named page.ts but should be page.tsx since it returns JSX. Next.js won't recognize it without the correct extension.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Follow App Router file conventions exactly. Use layout.tsx for shared UI and loading.tsx for suspense boundaries.

items.text: Are dynamic route segments properly typed and validated?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Dynamic routes like [id] or [...slug] receive params that should be validated before use. Params are always strings.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unvalidated route params can cause runtime errors or security issues. Passing a string where a number is expected silently breaks logic.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that dynamic params are validated (parsed as numbers, checked against allowed values, etc.) before being used in queries or rendering.
baseContent.exampleComment: params.id is used directly in a database query without validation. Parse and validate it first.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always validate dynamic route params. They are strings by default and need explicit parsing.

items.text: Is page metadata (title, description, og tags) properly defined?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each page should export a metadata object or generateMetadata function for SEO and social sharing.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing metadata hurts SEO rankings and makes shared links look unprofessional without proper titles and descriptions.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that pages export metadata or generateMetadata. Verify dynamic pages generate contextual metadata, not static defaults.
baseContent.exampleComment: This product page uses the default metadata. Export generateMetadata to set the title and description from the product data.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Define metadata for every page. Use generateMetadata for dynamic content.

items.text: Are parallel and intercepting routes used appropriately?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Parallel routes (@folder) render multiple pages simultaneously. Intercepting routes ((.)) show routes in modals while preserving context.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Misuse of these patterns creates confusing navigation. Overuse adds complexity without benefit.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that parallel routes have matching default.tsx files. Verify intercepting routes handle both modal and direct-navigation cases.
baseContent.exampleComment: This intercepting route works as a modal but breaks when navigated to directly. Add a full-page version at the original route.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use parallel and intercepting routes sparingly. Always handle the direct-navigation fallback.

items.text: Is 'use client' only added where actually needed?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Components are server components by default. 'use client' should only be added when the component uses hooks, event handlers, or browser APIs.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unnecessary 'use client' increases the client bundle size and prevents server-side optimizations like streaming and direct data access.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the component actually uses useState, useEffect, onClick, or browser APIs. If not, 'use client' is unnecessary.
baseContent.exampleComment: This component only renders props — it doesn't use any client features. Remove 'use client' to keep it as a server component.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Default to server components. Only add 'use client' when the component genuinely needs client-side interactivity.

items.text: Are server and client components composed correctly (client wrapping server via children)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Server components cannot be imported into client components directly. Pass server components as children or props to client components instead.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Importing a server component into a client component silently converts it to a client component, losing all server-side benefits.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that client components receive server components via children/props rather than importing them directly.
baseContent.exampleComment: This client component imports ServerData directly. Pass it as children instead: <ClientWrapper><ServerData /></ClientWrapper>.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Compose server components into client components via children props, never via direct imports.

items.text: Are props passed from server to client components serializable?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Props crossing the server-client boundary must be JSON-serializable. Functions, Dates, Maps, Sets, and class instances cannot be passed.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Non-serializable props cause runtime errors. Functions silently disappear, Dates become strings, and class instances lose their methods.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check props passed from server to client components. Ensure no functions, Dates, or complex objects cross the boundary.
baseContent.exampleComment: You're passing a Date object to a client component. Convert it to an ISO string and parse it on the client side.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Only pass JSON-serializable data across the server-client boundary.

items.text: Are server-only secrets and API keys kept out of client components?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Environment variables without NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix are server-only. Database connections, API keys, and secrets must never be used in client components.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Secrets used in client components are exposed in the browser bundle, visible to anyone inspecting the page source.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check client components for direct use of process.env variables (without NEXT_PUBLIC_), database clients, or secret keys.
baseContent.exampleComment: This client component references process.env.DATABASE_URL. Move this logic to a server component or API route.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never access secrets in client components. Use server components, server actions, or API routes instead.

items.text: Is data fetched in server components where possible (instead of client-side useEffect)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Server components can fetch data directly using async/await. This is faster and more secure than client-side fetching with useEffect.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Client-side fetching causes waterfalls, loading spinners, and exposes API endpoints. Server-side fetching sends ready HTML.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for useEffect + fetch patterns that could be replaced by server component data fetching.
baseContent.exampleComment: This page fetches data in useEffect. Since this is a server component, fetch the data directly with async/await at the top level.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Fetch data in server components. Reserve client-side fetching for real-time updates and user-triggered actions.

items.text: Are caching and revalidation strategies appropriate?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Next.js caches fetch requests by default. Use revalidate options (time-based or on-demand) to control freshness.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Over-caching shows stale data. Under-caching wastes resources and slows pages. Incorrect revalidation causes confusing behavior.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check fetch calls for cache/revalidate options. Verify dynamic data uses appropriate revalidation and static data is cached.
baseContent.exampleComment: This product price is cached indefinitely but prices change daily. Add { next: { revalidate: 3600 } } to refresh hourly.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Choose caching strategies based on data freshness requirements. Use revalidateTag for on-demand invalidation.

items.text: Are loading and error states handled with loading.tsx and error.tsx?

baseContent.whatItMeans: App Router provides loading.tsx for Suspense boundaries and error.tsx for error boundaries at the route segment level.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without loading states, users see blank pages during data fetching. Without error boundaries, errors crash the entire page.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that route segments with async data have loading.tsx files. Verify error.tsx exists for routes that can fail.
baseContent.exampleComment: This route fetches data but has no loading.tsx. Users see a blank page while data loads. Add a loading skeleton.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Add loading.tsx and error.tsx at appropriate route segments. Use Suspense for granular loading states.

items.text: Are server actions used correctly for mutations?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Server actions ('use server') handle form submissions and mutations. They should validate input, handle errors, and revalidate affected data.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Server actions without validation are vulnerable to injection. Missing revalidation leaves stale data after mutations.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that server actions validate all inputs, handle errors gracefully, and call revalidatePath/revalidateTag after mutations.
baseContent.exampleComment: This server action doesn't validate the form data before inserting into the database. Add input validation with zod or similar.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validate inputs in server actions. Always revalidate affected cache entries after mutations.

items.text: Are parallel data fetches used to avoid request waterfalls?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Sequential awaits create waterfalls. Use Promise.all() for independent fetches or fetch in parallel components with Suspense.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Sequential fetches add up latency. Three 200ms fetches in sequence take 600ms, but 200ms in parallel.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for sequential await statements where fetches don't depend on each other. Check for opportunities to parallelize.
baseContent.exampleComment: These two fetches are independent but awaited sequentially. Use Promise.all([fetchUser(), fetchPosts()]) to run them in parallel.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Parallelize independent data fetches with Promise.all or parallel Suspense boundaries.

items.text: Are images using next/image for automatic optimization?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The next/image component provides automatic image optimization, lazy loading, and responsive sizing.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unoptimized images are the biggest cause of slow pages. next/image reduces image size by 50-80% automatically.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for raw tags that should use from next/image. Verify width/height or fill props are set to prevent layout shift.
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses a raw tag for a hero image. Use next/image with priority prop for above-the-fold images.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use next/image for all images. Set priority on above-the-fold images.

items.text: Are heavy client components lazy-loaded with next/dynamic?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Large client components (charts, editors, maps) should be loaded with next/dynamic to reduce initial bundle size.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Loading a 500KB charting library on every page visit wastes bandwidth and delays interactivity.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for large client-side dependencies imported at the top level. Consider if they could be dynamically imported.
baseContent.exampleComment: This page imports a 300KB rich text editor at the top level. Use next/dynamic with { ssr: false } to lazy-load it.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Lazy-load heavy client components. Use next/dynamic for components that aren't needed immediately.

items.text: Are pages statically generated where possible?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Pages without dynamic data should be statically generated at build time. Use generateStaticParams for dynamic routes with known paths.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Static pages are served from CDN with near-zero latency. Dynamic rendering adds server processing time on every request.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if pages use cookies(), headers(), or searchParams unnecessarily, which force dynamic rendering.
baseContent.exampleComment: This page reads cookies() but doesn't use them. Remove the cookies() call to allow static generation.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Default to static generation. Only use dynamic rendering when the page truly depends on request-time data.

items.text: Is middleware scoped correctly with matchers?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Middleware runs on every request by default. Use config.matcher to limit it to specific routes.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unscoped middleware adds latency to every request including static assets, API calls, and images.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if middleware.ts has a matcher config. Verify it doesn't run on _next/static, _next/image, or favicon.ico.
baseContent.exampleComment: This middleware runs on every request including static files. Add a matcher to limit it to protected routes.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always scope middleware with matchers. Exclude static assets and public routes.

items.text: Do API route handlers validate input and return proper status codes?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Route handlers (route.ts) should validate request bodies, return appropriate HTTP status codes, and handle errors.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing validation leads to security vulnerabilities. Wrong status codes break client error handling and caching.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that route handlers validate input, use correct HTTP methods (GET, POST, etc.), and return proper status codes.
baseContent.exampleComment: This POST handler doesn't validate the request body. Add schema validation and return 400 for invalid input.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validate all inputs in route handlers. Return semantically correct HTTP status codes.

items.text: Is edge runtime used only when beneficial and compatible?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Edge runtime provides lower latency but has limitations (no Node.js APIs, limited npm packages, smaller memory).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Using edge runtime with incompatible dependencies causes build failures or runtime errors. Using it unnecessarily adds complexity.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for export const runtime = 'edge'. Verify the route doesn't use Node.js-only APIs like fs, crypto, or incompatible npm packages.
baseContent.exampleComment: This route handler uses edge runtime but imports bcrypt which requires Node.js. Remove the edge runtime or use a Web Crypto alternative.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use edge runtime for simple, latency-sensitive routes. Stick with Node.js runtime for complex logic.

items.text: Are server actions and API routes protected against CSRF?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Server actions have built-in CSRF protection, but custom API routes may need explicit protection. Verify Origin headers are checked.
baseContent.whyItMatters: CSRF attacks can trick authenticated users into performing unwanted actions like changing passwords or transferring funds.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that API routes verify Origin or Referer headers. Server actions are protected by default but verify custom fetch-based mutations.
baseContent.exampleComment: This API route accepts POST requests without CSRF protection. Verify the Origin header matches your domain.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use server actions (built-in CSRF protection) for mutations. Add explicit CSRF checks to custom API routes.

items.text: Is authentication checked in middleware AND in server components/actions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Middleware can redirect unauthenticated users, but server components and actions should also verify authentication. Defense in depth.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Middleware alone can be bypassed. Server-side auth checks prevent unauthorized data access even if middleware is misconfigured.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that server components fetching protected data verify the session. Check that server actions verify the user before mutations.
baseContent.exampleComment: This server action modifies user data but doesn't verify the session. Add auth check even though middleware protects the route.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Check auth in both middleware (for redirects) and server components/actions (for data protection).

items.text: Is dangerouslySetInnerHTML avoided or properly sanitized?

baseContent.whatItMeans: dangerouslySetInnerHTML renders raw HTML and bypasses React's XSS protection. If used, the HTML must be sanitized.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unsanitized HTML from user input or external sources enables XSS attacks that can steal sessions and credentials.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for dangerouslySetInnerHTML. Verify the HTML source is trusted or sanitized with a library like DOMPurify.
baseContent.exampleComment: This renders user-submitted HTML with dangerouslySetInnerHTML. Sanitize it with DOMPurify first.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Avoid dangerouslySetInnerHTML. If necessary, always sanitize the HTML first.
